paragraphs.body: I remember it like it was yesterday, A sunny day in New York City like any other. The grass was glistening after it rained, the air was humid, but the warm breeze did not let it bother me. The sun was shining bright as it slowly descends into the horizon. I look to my left, and I see the crowds of parents from both teams, cheering us on hoping we succeed. I look to my right as I see my coach demanding more from us and introducing his new on the spot instructions. I continued to look around and as I turn around and my eyes light up as the reflection of the sun shines at me from the World trade center. For a second, I stand still in awe of what’s in front of me. I was playing the beautiful game in a beautiful atmosphere. For a moment I was at peace, the muffled chanting from the parent’s was no longer audible. It truly was a euphoric moment. Unfortunately disrupted by a loud, annoying whistle blown mere feet away from me, it only meant one thing, the game has begun. I quickly turn around and face the opponents. Their charging footsteps can be heard by the way their cleats rummaging through the muddy field followed by the thud made by the ball when it’s been kicked. Reality began to sink in, but my mind was disinterested, as if I had something important to attend to right after the game. But a 14-year-old does not have that much responsibility, so I began to focus on what was happening around me. The way my teammates shouted reminded me to communicate and the speed of which the ball was played told me to get up to speed and finally my coaches yell put me in motion. I ran to my teammate and demanded the ball but unbeknownst to me my legs were in motion, but my mind was not. Every step I take all I could think about was don’t mess this up. It was louder than anything going on around me, it was so loud that I didn’t hear the charging footsteps that followed me. As I receive the ball on a half turn ready to face my opponent up and make my decision, I noticed I was too late. A decision was made before mine. A kid about twice the size of myself clattered right into me. The thud that the cleat had on my bone was more audible than anything my mind could muster. I fell to the floor in pain and agony, screaming so loud that even New Jersey was alarmed. Not long after two senior figures came rushing to my side, but I didn’t hear what they were asking me. I looked around and what I saw in the faces of the kids around me spoke louder than words could. After I arrived at the hospital I began to worry, not because my 14-year-old self could explain the gravity in of the injury medical terms. But because my mom was extremely worried and that’s unlike her. Eventually doctors rushed in full body attire, masks covering their faces and hairnet’s covering their normal look. I was pushed out of the room on my bed s quickly I thought I got kidnapped. Next thing I remember was the doctor informing my mom that the function of the leg cannot be determined yet.

annotations.comment: keep your verb tense consistent throughout!

annotations.text: Next thing I remember was the doctor informing my mom that the function of the leg cannot be determined yet.

paragraphs.body: My mother was never a fan of western medicine and much less of me wasting money. I mean you would think when you need something badly specifically for medical purposes you would get an excuse but of course that was not the case. And believe me I did not hear the end of it at home. In an aggressive tone she goes on a monologue about how I should have been more careful and that we cannot be wasting money like this. As if I was old enough to make income. At that moment the pain was so bad I didn’t even care to argue but just agreed and hoped for it to end soon. Eventually she stormed back to her room, and I was left with a tense atmosphere in the household but peace for my ears. Coming from a Hispanic family you really learn to save as much as you can and trust me you’d never forget it. The monologue she gave was her way of writing waste on my cast and money on my crutches. Nevertheless, I was happy I would be okay. Days went on and the sympathy treatment I got from my family began to decrease. So, I started to test my ability to function on my own. First, I made my own food, and even though my success rate wasn’t great it was a start. Then I walked the dog and thankfully did not lose him, but it was a defeat that after two blocks I had to turn around and cut the walk short. Lastly, I started carrying packages seemingly too big for my current functionality. Challenges were quite common those days to the point that it felt as if I needed a part time assistant. One that could do the things that were made inconvenient by my broken leg easier. It would sure have made my life easier because some of the things I could not even do if I tried. I began to grow impatient with my recovery. How could I be so useless. I felt disabled and in a sense I temporarily was. I had to abide by rules that limit my ability to function normally. But one day I had to conquer something new. An outdoor excursion to Target.

annotations.text: My mother was never a fan of western medicine and much less of me wasting money.

paragraphs.body: This adventure would really put me to the test. My first outdoor activity without anyone by my side. As I walk through the doors, I get a bit overwhelmed. Not only because I cover a lot of the entranceway causing a backup but also because I don’t know what to get a cart or a basket. I stand there awkwardly hoping no one sympathizes with me because it made me feel different. As I contemplate a decision, I see something shiny in my peripheral. A lip stick like red emphasized by the overhead light shining off its bumper. It was an in-store scooter. Suddenly, I found my choice and I scurry on over to meet my ride. As I move closer, I got a whiff of something that must have died on that seat, so I skip past the first and go to the second. Which wasn’t much better considering the massive hole on the seat. At least the third, didn’t smell great and the seat was hard, but it would do. I began the engine, and I began to move but the wheel was kind of messed up, like one of those shopping carts with the wheel that resemble the inflated mascots outside of a car dealership. Regardless I counteract the wheel left sided movements with the rightward steering. Immediately I noticed how for people who need the most help getting around they sure don’t have it easy. I continue on looking through the aisles, but countless times I didn’t get the item I wanted because I felt rushed when someone was behind me. Primarily because the scooter took up the whole aisle. I felt really uncomfortable, I just felt like I didn’t fit in. Furthermore, the stares I got were as descriptive as words can be. The way you are looked at when you cannot function like normal is one of sympathy or inadequacy. It made me feel like I was lesser even if my injury was temporary. The scooter at that point acted like a bright red sign saying I can’t function on my own. Surely my mom would have laughed. But the anxiety you get when that many people deny you any feeling of peace is terrible. I can’t imagine someone who is permanent disabled having to suffer like this on their day to day lives. Especially on a scooter, because you can hear the old run-down engine and when you reverse it sounds like a fire alarm went off. The conditions of the vehicle are uncomfortable, its dirty with stains on the seat, smells like someone used it as a bathroom and is barely big enough to fit in the aisle. Everything about my scooter is a giant reminder that I am not able to function alone. The inadequacy became too much, and I had to leave. My hands begin to tremble on the steering mechanism but all I can hear is the rundown motor that sounds ten times louder to me when you are trying to be inconspicuous. As I start booking it for the exit I hit an aisle. I felt so embarrassed and now everything was exacerbated, in my eyes the situation was dire, I either got out immediately or I’d die from an anxiety attack. My inability to function was so bad I was an inconvenience to everyone and when I left the symbol of the scooter stuck with me because in my eyes they transformed into my crutches.

annotations.text: can’t imagine someone who is permanent disabled having to suffer like this on their day to day lives.
The way you are looked at when you cannot function like normal is one of sympathy or inadequacy. It made me feel like I was lesser even if my injury was temporary.
annotations.comment: Interesting moments--do these insight reveal something about what's at the heart of this essay?

paragraphs.body: When people use the word function, they usually talk about how effective it is. But people forget to acknowledge how no matter the ability to function there is always some function that is missing. Nothing is perfect and that’s normal, but when you can’t function on your own in our current society that makes us different. And the way people treat you shows a person in need that their different is bad. This negative connotation is symbolized through medication, scooters, crutches etc. And people cannot hide their lack of function. But the terrifying fact is that out in the world there is so many people in need that can’t function well. But in addition to the unfortunate circumstance that they find themselves in, there is people who judge, treat them different, tease and so on. There should be more care and awareness for people who cannot function on their own. They should not be treated different for any reason, no one is perfect but by experiencing both sides of the function spectrum I now notice how important it is for people who have more function than others, to offer some of theirs. The world needs more interdependence, and it all starts with you today.

annotations.text: There should be more care and awareness for people who cannot function on their
